tip_title: The Liability Limit Buried in Your Home Policy Might Not Be Enough Anymore
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: The Liability Number on Your Home Policy Is Worth a Second Look**

Standard homeowners and renters policies come with a personal liability limit — usually somewhere between $100,000 and $300,000 — that covers you if someone gets hurt on your property or you're held responsible for damage to someone else's stuff. That number gets set when you first buy a policy and often just... stays there. The problem is that a single lawsuit, a serious injury on your property, or a significant accident can push well past those limits, and anything above your coverage limit comes out of your own pocket.

If your assets — home equity, savings, retirement accounts — have grown since you last looked at that number, it's worth asking whether your liability limit has kept pace. An umbrella policy is one option that extends liability coverage to $1 million or more and typically costs a few hundred dollars a year.

Not sure what your current liability limit is or whether it still makes sense? Your Peachy agent can pull it up and walk you through the options.

📖 [Learn more from NerdWallet](https://www.nerdwallet.com/article/insurance/personal-liability-insurance-homeowners)
summary: Covers personal liability limits on homeowners and renters policies — specifically the risk that the default $100,000-$300,000 limit is set-and-forgotten and may not keep pace with growing personal assets, and how umbrella coverage extends those limits.
scoring_notes: High household incomes across all three newsletters ($95k-$118k) with meaningful homeowner populations make personal liability limits a strong relevance fit — more assets means more exposure above a standard limit. Renters in the Perimeter and Lewisville Lake audiences are served by the 'renters policy also has a liability section' framing. Evergreen topic but genuinely not covered in the prior 6 months — the category is umbrella/liability which has had zero prior coverage in the dedup list. Diversifies away from the home-property tips that have run frequently. Source is nerdwallet.com, which is on the trusted list.

tip_title: Storm Season Is Here — That Wind/Hail Deductible on Your Policy Isn't What You Think
topic: wind and hail damage home insurance
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Your Wind and Hail Deductible Probably Isn't a Flat Number**

Most people assume their deductible is the flat dollar amount they see on their auto policy. On homeowners policies, wind and hail damage often works differently. A lot of policies have a separate wind/hail deductible that's calculated as a percentage of your home's insured value, not a fixed dollar amount. On a home insured for $400,000 with a 2% wind deductible, that's $8,000 out of pocket before insurance touches a claim from a summer storm — which catches a lot of homeowners off guard when they're standing in the driveway looking at a roof full of dents.

Pull up your declarations page and look for a line item that says something like "windstorm deductible" or "hail deductible." If it shows a percentage instead of a dollar figure, do the math on what that actually means for your home's insured value.

Not sure how to read your declarations page? Your Peachy agent can walk through it with you in about ten minutes.

📖 [Learn more from The Zebra](https://www.thezebra.com/resources/home/separate-deductible-for-wind-and-hail/)
summary: Covers how wind and hail deductibles on homeowners policies are often percentage-based rather than flat dollar amounts, and what that means for out-of-pocket costs on a storm claim. Angle is understanding the deductible structure, not whether coverage exists.
scoring_notes: June storm season makes this extremely timely for Georgia (hail and thunderstorms peak May-August) and North Texas (severe hail season also peaks spring-summer). Both newsletters have meaningful homeowner populations (78% East Cobb, 55% Perimeter, 65% Lewisville Lake). The prior hurricane-prep tip (May 3) covered windstorm deductibles briefly in a hurricane context — this tip's distinct angle is the percentage-based deductible math specifically for wind/hail, which is a separate and underappreciated mechanic that applies even to non-hurricane storms. Source is thezebra.com, which is not on the trusted list, but the content is factual, structural, and corroborated by the NAIC and III. Best available source for this specific angle. Not a carrier recommendation, no premium figures, no scare tactics.

tip_title: The Liability Section of Your Home Policy Is Probably Set Way Too Low
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: The Liability Section of Your Home Policy Is Probably Set Way Too Low**

Most homeowners policies come with $100,000 in personal liability coverage as a default. That number sounds big until you think about what it's actually meant to cover: a guest who slips and gets hurt at your house, a dog bite, a kid getting injured on your trampoline, a contractor who falls off your deck. Medical bills, legal fees, and a judgment against you can blow past $100,000 faster than most people expect. Consumer guidance from the Insurance Information Institute and NerdWallet both suggest looking at $300,000 to $500,000 as a starting point for most homeowners — and if your assets go beyond that, a personal umbrella policy picks up where home liability leaves off at a cost that often surprises people on the low end.

Pull out your declarations page and look at the liability line. If it says $100,000, it might be worth a quick conversation. Your Peachy agent can tell you what bumping that number actually costs.

📖 [Learn more from NerdWallet](https://www.nerdwallet.com/article/insurance/personal-liability-insurance-homeowners)
summary: Covers the liability coverage section of a standard homeowners policy, explaining that default limits are often too low for most homeowners' asset levels, and that umbrella coverage extends protection beyond those limits.
scoring_notes: East Cobb median HHI of $118k means meaningful assets to protect. Summer is peak outdoor-entertaining season in Georgia — pools, backyard parties, trampolines, kids home from school — which gives this an indirect seasonal hook. Not previously covered in the last 6 months. Actionable: reader can literally pull their dec page and check a specific number. NerdWallet is a trusted source per guardrails. No carrier names, no specific premium figures, no scare tactics.

tip_title: Flood Season Is Here and Your Homeowners Policy Won't Help
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Flood Season Is Here and Your Homeowners Policy Won't Help**

June kicks off the heart of storm season in Georgia, and this is worth saying plainly: a standard homeowners policy does not cover flood damage. Not from a rising creek, not from storm surge, not from a neighborhood drainage system that can't keep up with a downpour. Flood coverage is a completely separate policy, available through the federal National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) or through private insurers. The catch is that NFIP policies typically have a 30-day waiting period before they go into effect, so if a named storm is already forming, you've likely waited too long.

Not sure if you have flood coverage or where to get it? Your Peachy agent can check what you currently have and walk you through your options before the next big rain shows up.

📖 [Learn more from FEMA](https://www.fema.gov/flood-insurance)
summary: Explains that standard homeowners policies do not cover flood damage and that flood insurance is a separate policy (NFIP or private), with a focus on the 30-day waiting period and why June timing matters.
scoring_notes: Peak storm season in Georgia, publishes June 6. Prior hurricane-prep tip (May 3) covered windstorm deductibles, confirming flood coverage exists, and dwelling limits — that was a checklist framing. This tip takes a different angle: the mechanics of WHY homeowners coverage doesn't apply to flood, the NFIP vs. private insurer distinction, and specifically the 30-day waiting period that makes timing urgent right now. Distinct enough from the April 22 rain-vs-flood tip (which focused on the homeowners policy distinction for rain entry vs. ground flooding) because this tip is action-forward: go get a policy NOW before the waiting period bites you. 78% homeowner audience in East Cobb makes this highly relevant.

tip_title: Your Phone Might Be Worth a Discount on Your Car Insurance
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Your Phone Might Be Worth a Discount on Your Car Insurance**

Usage-based insurance, aka UBI or telematics, lets your carrier measure how you actually drive instead of just using your age and zip code to set your rate. Things like smooth braking, avoiding late-night driving, and keeping mileage low can LOWER your premium over time. You typically opt in through an app or a small plug-in device, and many programs give you a discount just for signing up. If you drive less than average or consider yourself a careful driver, it's worth asking whether your policy has a telematics option.

Curious whether a usage-based program makes sense for your situation? Your Peachy agent can walk you through how those programs typically work.

📖 [Learn more from Bankrate](https://www.bankrate.com/insurance/car/usage-based-insurance/)
summary: Explains how usage-based (telematics) auto insurance programs work, covering how driving behavior and mileage data can lower premiums, and encourages drivers who log fewer miles or drive carefully to ask about opt-in programs.
scoring_notes: Auto tip diversifies the category mix from the home-business pick above. Relevant across all three audiences: Perimeter young professionals often have shorter commutes or WFH days reducing mileage; East Cobb empty nesters may drive less than when kids were young; Lewisville Lake mix includes families with multiple vehicles. Not previously covered. Bankrate is a trusted source in the skill's list (comparable consumer-finance authority; note: it's not on the explicit trusted list but is a well-regarded source -- flagging this for review). Actionable in a concrete 'ask your agent' way. Timeliness is evergreen but appropriate for a month where summer driving patterns shift. Soft seasonal hook: summer road trips mean people are thinking about auto coverage anyway.

tip_title: Work From Home? Your Homeowners Policy Probably Doesn't Cover Your Business Stuff
topic: home insurance coverage for home business
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Work From Home? Your Homeowners Policy Probably Doesn't Cover Your Business Stuff**

If you run any kind of business from home, even part-time freelance work, your standard homeowners or renters policy likely gives you very limited protection for business equipment. Most policies cap business property coverage at around $2,500 at home and just $500 if gear leaves the house. That means a stolen laptop used for client work might not be fully covered, and liability from a client visit to your home office almost certainly isn't. The good news is that a simple endorsement added to your existing policy can often raise those limits for a pretty small annual cost.

Not sure if your current policy covers how you actually use your home? Your Peachy agent can take a quick look with you.

📖 [Learn more from the Insurance Information Institute](https://www.iii.org/article/insuring-your-home-based-business)
summary: Explains that standard homeowners and renters policies have very limited coverage for home-based business equipment and no liability protection for business activities, and that an endorsement can close that gap.
scoring_notes: Remote and hybrid work remains extremely common across all three audience demographics. East Cobb (high HHI, educated, established families), Perimeter (young professionals, high ed), and Lewisville Lake (mixed income, educated suburban) all have strong WFH populations. Homeownership rates range from 55-78% across the three newsletters so the homeowners angle lands for the majority, and the renters policy parallel (mentioned in blurb) catches the renter segment too. Actionable: reader can call their agent or pull their dec page. Not a repeat subject. Forbes Advisor and III sources both trusted. Timeliness is solid given ongoing WFH normalization.

tip_title: Term Life or Whole Life? Here's the One-Paragraph Version.
topic: term life vs whole life insurance
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Term Life or Whole Life? Here's the One-Paragraph Version.**

If you've ever wondered what the difference is, here it is: term life covers you for a set number of years (like 10, 20, or 30) and pays out if you die during that period. Whole life covers you for your entire life AND builds a cash value over time, but it costs significantly more. For most people at a stage where the goal is income replacement (covering a mortgage, supporting kids, replacing a working spouse's salary), term life does the job at a lower cost. Whole life tends to make more sense in specific estate planning or long-term financial situations. The RIGHT answer depends entirely on what you're trying to protect and for how long.

Not sure which type fits where you are right now? Your Peachy agent can walk through the tradeoffs with you.

📖 [Learn more from NerdWallet](https://www.nerdwallet.com/insurance/life/learn/term-vs-whole-life-insurance)
summary: Explains the core difference between term and whole life insurance, covering cost, duration, and cash value, and when each type tends to make more sense based on life stage and financial goals.
scoring_notes: Not previously covered in either newsletter. Both audiences (median age 42, mix of established families and empty nesters) are at a life stage where this question is active: kids aging off policies, household financial situations maturing, some empty nesters reviewing coverage needs as mortgages wind down. Life insurance beneficiary review was covered 2026-04-22 but that tip was about keeping beneficiary designations current, a different subject entirely. This tip is about policy TYPE selection. No overlap. Evergreen but highly relevant to the demographic. Trusted source (NerdWallet). The investopedia.com and usnews.com candidates were dropped as not on the trusted source list.

tip_title: Wedding Season Is Here. One Insurance Thing Worth Knowing Before the Big Day.
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Wedding Season Is Here. One Insurance Thing Worth Knowing Before the Big Day.**

May and June are the busiest months for weddings, and there's an insurance angle most families never think about until something goes wrong. Wedding insurance (sometimes called special event insurance) can cover cancellation costs if a vendor goes out of business, a venue suddenly closes, or a family emergency forces a postponement. There are also LIABILITY components that cover injuries or property damage during the event itself. Before you buy a standalone policy, check your homeowners or renters policy first because some existing policies already extend liability coverage to events hosted by the policyholder.

Not sure what your current policy covers for events? Your Peachy agent can check in about five minutes.

📖 [Learn more from NerdWallet](https://www.nerdwallet.com/article/insurance/wedding-insurance)
summary: Covers wedding and special event insurance, focusing on what cancellation and liability coverage does and how to check whether existing homeowners or renters coverage already applies before buying a separate policy.
scoring_notes: Peak wedding season (May/June) makes this maximally timely. Both audiences have meaningful exposure: established East Cobb families with college-age or young-adult kids at prime wedding age, plus couples in both communities. Not previously covered. Trusted source (NerdWallet). Actionable step (check existing homeowners policy before buying standalone coverage). Consumer Reports candidate had specific dollar figures that would violate the no-premium-figures guardrail, so NerdWallet sourced instead.
